title: Doubles
subtitle: Trinidadian curried chickpea bara
excerpt: Two small, soft fried flatbreads (bara) loaded with curried chickpeas (channa), then dressed with a combination of cucumber chutney, tamarind sauce, mango chutney, coconut chutney, and pepper sauce. Doubles is the defining street food of Trinidad — eaten for breakfast, before dawn, from roadside doubles vendors, at a price so low it is a daily staple for millions. The bara is the delivery system; the channa is the filling; the chutneys are the seasoning.
sourceNotes: Doubles was created in Trinidad in the 1930s by Emamool Deen and his wife Rahiman, who began selling the first version of the dish in Princes Town. The name 'doubles' refers to the two bara (flatbreads) used — a single bara with filling was called a 'single'. The dish draws on the culinary tradition of the large Indian community in Trinidad, descendants of indentured workers who came after Emancipation.

recipe.batchNotes: The channa keeps in the fridge for 3 days. Make bara fresh for serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The channa can be made a day ahead. The bara dough can be made up to 2 hours ahead.

content.text: The bara is a soft, slightly chewy flatbread — not crispy. It is fried very briefly in shallow oil, just long enough to cook through and develop a light golden colour on each side. It should be supple enough to fold around the channa without cracking. The dough must rest for at least 30 minutes or the bara will be tough.

The channa (curried chickpeas) should be well-seasoned and slightly saucy — not dry. The Trinidadian curry spice mix is flavoured with shadow beni (culantro), which is hard to find in Britain. Coriander is the closest available substitute and gives a similar freshness.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a bowl. Add 180 ml of warm water and mix to a soft, sticky dough. Knead for 5 minutes — the dough should be softer than bread dough. Cover and rest for 30 minutes.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{curry-powder}}, and {{cumin-ground}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{scotch-bonnet}} and the {{chickpeas}}. Pour in 200 ml of water and stir. Simmer for 15 minutes until the sauce has thickened and the chickpeas are very well seasoned. Add the {{coriander}} and {{sea-salt-fine}}. The channa should be moist but not soupy. Keep warm.

Divide the bara dough into 24 pieces (2 per serving). Wet your hands with oil and press each piece into a thin round about 10 cm across — do not use a rolling pin, use your fingers and palms. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 170°C in a wide pan. Fry each bara for about 30 seconds per side until lightly golden and puffed. They cook very fast. Drain on kitchen paper. To serve, stack two bara and spoon on the channa. Add mango chutney, pepper sauce, and coriander chutney to taste.

items.cause: Dough not rested or fried too long
items.fix: Rest the dough for the full 30 minutes. Bara should only take 30 seconds per side — more than this and they become tough

items.cause: Dough too thick or oil not hot enough
items.fix: Press the bara as thin as possible — no more than 3 mm. If the oil is below 165°C, the bara will absorb oil and not puff

items.cause: Too little water or cooked too long
items.fix: Add water a tablespoon at a time. The channa should be moist enough to scoop without being wet

content.text: Doubles is the national street food of Trinidad. It is eaten at breakfast by more Trinidadians than any other single food, bought from doubles vendors who set up before dawn and often sell out by 9 am. In Port of Spain and across Trinidad the doubles vendor is a fixture of daily life — the stalls are usually unmanned in the sense that the vendor works at high speed, the customer says 'slight, medium, or heavy' for the pepper sauce, and the transaction is over in under a minute. In Britain doubles appears at Trinidadian restaurants and food events, made with the same basic structure but rarely with the full array of traditional chutneys.
